title: XXXIII: The Plumstead Foxes
html:
XXXIII

The Plumstead Foxes

The letters had been brought into the breakfast-parlour at Plumstead Rectory one morning, and the archdeacon had inspected them all, and then thrown over to his wife her share of the spoilÔªø‚Äîas was the custom of the house. As to most of Mrs.¬ÝGrantly‚Äôs letters, he never made any further inquiry. To letters from her sister, the dean‚Äôs wife, he was profoundly indifferent, and rarely made any inquiry as to those which were directed in writing with which he was not familiar. But there were others as to which, as Mrs.¬ÝGrantly knew, he would be sure to ask her questions if she did not show them. No note ever reached her from Lady Hartletop as to which he was not curious, and yet Lady Hartletop‚Äôs notes very seldom contained much that was of interest. Now, on this morning, there came a letter which, as a matter of course, Mrs.¬ÝGrantly read at breakfast, and which, she knew, would not be allowed to disappear without inquiry. Nor, indeed, did she wish to keep the letter from her husband. It was too important to be so treated. But she would have been glad to gain time to think in what spirit she would discuss the contents of the letterÔªø‚Äîif only such time might be allowed to her. But the archdeacon would allow her no time. ‚ÄúWhat does Henry say, my dear?‚Äù he asked, before the breakfast things had been taken away.

‚ÄúWhat does he say? Well; he saysÔªø‚Äî. I‚Äôll give you his letter to read by-and-by.‚Äù

‚ÄúAnd why not now?‚Äù

‚ÄúI thought I‚Äôd read it again myself, first.‚Äù

‚ÄúBut if you have read it, I suppose you know what‚Äôs in it?‚Äù

‚ÄúNot very clearly, as yet. However, there it is.‚Äù She knew very well that when she had once been asked for it, no peace would be allowed to her till he had seen it. And, alas! there was not much probability of peace in the house for some time after he should see it.

The archdeacon read the three or four first lines in silenceÔªø‚Äîand then he burst out. ‚ÄúHe has, has he? Then, by heavensÔªø‚Äî‚Äù

‚ÄúStop, dearest; stop,‚Äù said his wife, rising from her chair and coming over to him; ‚Äúdo not say words which you will surely repent.‚Äù

‚ÄúI will say words which shall make him repent. He shall never have from me a son‚Äôs portion.‚Äù

‚ÄúDo not make threats in anger. Do not! You know that it is wrong. If he has offended you, say nothing about itÔªø‚Äîeven to yourselfÔªø‚Äîas to threatened punishments, till you can judge of the offence in cool blood.‚Äù

‚ÄúI am cool,‚Äù said the archdeacon.

‚ÄúNo, my dear; no; you are angry. And you have not even read his letter through.‚Äù

‚ÄúI will read his letter.‚Äù

‚ÄúYou will see that the marriage is not imminent. It may be that even yet it will never take place. The young lady has refused him.‚Äù

‚ÄúPsha!‚Äù

‚ÄúYou will see that she has done so. He tells us so himself. And she has behaved very properly.‚Äù

‚ÄúWhy has she refused him?‚Äù

‚ÄúThere can be no doubt about the reason. She feels that, with this charge hanging over her father, she is not in a position to become the wife of any gentleman. You cannot but respect her for that.‚Äù

Then the archdeacon finished his son‚Äôs letter, uttering sundry interjections and ejaculations as he did so.

‚ÄúOf course; I knew it. I understood it all,‚Äù he said at last. ‚ÄúI‚Äôve nothing to do with the girl. I don‚Äôt care whether she be good or bad.‚Äù

‚ÄúOh, my dear!‚Äù

‚ÄúI care not at allÔªø‚Äîwith reference to my own concerns. Of course I would wish that the daughter of a neighbouring clergymanÔªø‚Äîthat the daughter of any neighbourÔªø‚Äîthat the daughter of anyone whatsoeverÔªø‚Äîshould be good rather than bad. But as regards Henry and me, and our mutual relation, her goodness can make no difference. Let her be another Grizel, and still such a marriage must estrange him from me, and me from him.‚Äù

‚ÄúBut she has refused him.‚Äù

‚ÄúYes; and what does he say?Ôªø‚Äîthat he has told her that he will not accept her refusal. Of course we know what it all means. The girl I am not judging. The girl I will not judge. But my own son, to whom I have ever done a father‚Äôs duty with a father‚Äôs affectionate indulgenceÔªø‚Äîhim I will judge. I have warned him, and he declares himself to be careless of my warning. I shall take no notice of this letter. I shall neither write to him about it, or speak to him about it. But I charge you to write to him, and tell him that if he does this thing he shall not have a child‚Äôs portion from me. It is not that I will shorten that which would have been his; but he shall haveÔªø‚Äînothing!‚Äù Then, having spoken these words with a solemnity which for the moment silenced his wife, he got up and left the room. He left the room and closed the door, but, before he had gone half the length of the hall towards his own study, he returned and addressed his wife again. ‚ÄúYou understand my instructions, I hope?‚Äù

‚ÄúWhat instructions?‚Äù

‚ÄúThat you write to Henry and tell him what I say.‚Äù

‚ÄúI will speak again to you about it by-and-by.‚Äù

‚ÄúI will speak no more about itÔªø‚Äînot a word more. Let there be not a word more said, but oblige me by doing as I ask you.‚Äù

Then he was again about to leave the room, but she stopped him. ‚ÄúWait a moment, my dear.‚Äù

‚ÄúWhy should I wait?‚Äù

‚ÄúThat you may listen to me. Surely you will do that, when I ask you. I will write to Henry, of course, if you bid me; and I will give him your message, whatever it may be; but not today, my dear.‚Äù

‚ÄúWhy not today?‚Äù

‚ÄúBecause the sun shall go down upon your wrath before I become its messenger. If you choose to write today yourself, I cannot help it. I cannot hinder you. If I am to write to him on your behalf I will take my instructions from you tomorrow morning. When tomorrow morning comes you will not be angry with me because of the delay.‚Äù

The archdeacon was by no means satisfied; but he knew his wife too well, and himself too well, and the world too well, to insist on the immediate gratification of his passion. Over his bosom‚Äôs mistress he did exercise a certain marital controlÔªø‚Äîwhich was, for instance, quite sufficiently fixed to enable him to look down with thorough contempt on such a one as Bishop Proudie; but he was not a despot who could exact a passive obedience to every fantasy. His wife would not have written the letter for him on that day, and he knew very well that she would not do so. He knew also that she was right;Ôªø‚Äîand yet he regretted his want of power. His anger at the present moment was very hotÔªø‚Äîso hot that he wished to wreak it. He knew that it would cool before the morrow;Ôªø‚Äîand, no doubt, knew also theoretically, that it would be most fitting that it should cool. But not the less was it a matter of regret to him that so much good hot anger should be wasted, and that he could not have his will of his disobedient son while it lasted. He might, no doubt, have written himself, but to have done so would not have suited him. Even in his anger he could not have written to his son without using the ordinary terms of affection, and in his anger he could not bring himself to use those terms. ‚ÄúYou will find that I shall be of the same mind tomorrowÔªø‚Äîexactly,‚Äù he said to his wife. ‚ÄúI have resolved about it long since; and it is not likely that I shall change in a day.‚Äù Then he went out, about his parish, intending to continue to think of his son‚Äôs iniquity, so that he might keep his anger hotÔªø‚Äîred hot. Then he remembered that the evening would come, and that he would say his prayers; and he shook his head in regretÔªø‚Äîin a regret of which he was only half conscious, though it was very keen, and which he did not attempt to analyzeÔªø‚Äîas he reflected that his rage would hardly be able to survive that ordeal. How common with us it is to repine that the devil is not stronger over us than he is.

The archdeacon, who was a very wealthy man, had purchased a property in Plumstead, contiguous to the glebe-land, and had thus come to exercise in the parish the double duty of rector and squire. And of this estate in Barsetshire, which extended beyond the confines of Plumstead into the neighbouring parish of Eiderdown, and which comprised also an outlying farm in the parish of StogpingumÔªø‚ÄîStoke Pinguium would have been the proper name had not barbarous Saxon tongues clipped it of its proper proportionsÔªø‚Äîhe had always intended that his son Henry should enjoy the inheritance. There was other property, both in land and in money, for his elder son, and other again for the maintenance of his wifeÔªø‚Äîfor the archdeacon‚Äôs father had been for many years Bishop of Barchester, and such a bishopric as that of Barchester had been in those days was worth money. Of his intention in this respect he had never spoken in plain language to either of his sons; but the major had for the last year or two enjoyed the shooting of the Barsetshire covers, giving what orders he pleased about the game; and the father had encouraged him to take something like the management of the property into his hands. There might be some fifteen hundred acres of it altogether, and the archdeacon had rejoiced over it with his wife scores of times, saying that there was many a squire in the county whose elder son would never find himself half so well placed as would his own younger son. Now there was a string of narrow woods called Plumstead Coppices which ran from a point near the church right across the parish, dividing the archdeacon‚Äôs land from the Ullathorne estate, and these coppices, or belts of woodland, belonged to the archdeacon. On the morning of which we are speaking, the archdeacon, mounted on his cob, still thinking of his son‚Äôs iniquity and of his own fixed resolve to punish him as he had said that he would punish him, opened with his whip a woodland gate, from which a green muddy lane led through the trees up to the house of his gamekeeper. The man‚Äôs wife was ill, and in his ordinary way of business the archdeacon was about to call and ask after her health. At the door of the cottage he found the man, who was woodman as well as gamekeeper, and was responsible for fences and faggots, as well as for foxes and pheasants‚Äô eggs.

‚ÄúHow‚Äôs Martha, Flurry?‚Äù said the archdeacon.

‚ÄúThanking your reverence, she be a deal improved since the mistress was hereÔªø‚Äîlast Tuesday it was, I think.‚Äù

‚ÄúI‚Äôm glad of that. It was only rheumatism, I suppose?‚Äù

‚ÄúJust a tich of fever with it, your reverence, the doctor said.‚Äù

‚ÄúTell her I was asking after it. I won‚Äôt mind getting down today, as I am rather busy. She has had what she wanted from the house?‚Äù

‚ÄúThe mistress has been very good in that way. She always is, God bless her!‚Äù

‚ÄúGood day to you, Flurry. I‚Äôll ask Mr.¬ÝSims to come and read to her a bit this afternoon, or tomorrow morning.‚Äù The archdeacon kept two curates, and Mr.¬ÝSims was one of them.

‚ÄúShe‚Äôll take it very kindly, your reverence. But while you are here, sir, there‚Äôs just a word I‚Äôd like to say. I didn‚Äôt happen to catch Mr.¬ÝHenry when he was here the other day.‚Äù

‚ÄúNever mind Mr.¬ÝHenry; what is it you have to say?‚Äù

‚ÄúI do think, I do indeed, sir, that Mr.¬ÝThorne‚Äôs man ain‚Äôt dealing fairly along of the foxes. I wouldn‚Äôt say a word about it, only that Mr.¬ÝHenry is so particular.‚Äù

‚ÄúWhat about the foxes? What is he doing with the foxes?‚Äù

‚ÄúWell, sir, he‚Äôs a trapping on ‚Äôem. He is, indeed, your reverence. I wouldn‚Äôt speak if I warn‚Äôt well nigh mortial sure.‚Äù

Now the archdeacon had never been a hunting man, though in his early days many a clergyman had been in the habit of hunting without losing his clerical character by doing so; but he had lived all his life among gentlemen in a hunting county, and had his own very strong ideas about the trapping of foxes. Foxes first, and pheasants afterwards, had always been the rule with him as to any land of which he himself had had the management. And no man understood better than he did how to deal with keepers as to this matter of fox-preserving, or knew better that keepers will in truth obey not the words of their employers, but their sympathies. ‚ÄúWish them to have foxes, and pay them, and they will have them,‚Äù Mr.¬ÝSowerby of Chaldicotes used to say, and he in his day was reckoned to be the best preserver of foxes in Barsetshire. ‚ÄúTell them to have them, and don‚Äôt wish it, and pay them well, and you won‚Äôt have a fox to interfere with your game. I don‚Äôt care what a man says to me, I can read it all like a book when I see his covers drawn.‚Äù That was what poor Mr.¬ÝSowerby of Chaldicotes used to say, and the archdeacon had heard him say it a score of times, and had learned the lesson. But now his heart was not with the foxesÔªø‚Äîand especially not with the foxes on behalf of his son Henry. ‚ÄúI can‚Äôt have any meddling with Mr.¬ÝThorne,‚Äù he said; ‚ÄúI can‚Äôt, and I won‚Äôt.‚Äù

‚ÄúBut I don‚Äôt suppose it can be Mr.¬ÝThorne‚Äôs order, your reverence; and Mr.¬ÝHenry is so particular.‚Äù

‚ÄúOf course it isn‚Äôt Mr.¬ÝThorne‚Äôs order. Mr.¬ÝThorne has been a hunting man all his life.‚Äù

‚ÄúBut he have guv‚Äô up now, your reverence. He ain‚Äôt a hunted these two years.‚Äù

‚ÄúI‚Äôm sure he wouldn‚Äôt have the foxes trapped.‚Äù

‚ÄúNot if he knowed it, he wouldn‚Äôt, your reverence. A gentleman of the likes of him, who‚Äôs been a hunting over fifty year, wouldn‚Äôt do the likes of that; but the foxes is trapped, and Mr.¬ÝHenry‚Äôll be a putting it on me if I don‚Äôt speak out. They is Plumstead foxes, too; and a vixen was trapped just across the field yonder, in Goshall Springs, no later than yesterday morning.‚Äù Flurry was now thoroughly in earnest; and, indeed, the trapping of a vixen in February is a serious thing.

‚ÄúGoshall Springs don‚Äôt belong to me,‚Äù said the archdeacon.

‚ÄúNo, your reverence; they‚Äôre on the Ullathorne property. But a word from your reverence would do it. Mr.¬ÝHenry thinks more of the foxes than anything. The last word he told me was that it would break his heart if he saw the coppices drawn blank.‚Äù

‚ÄúThen he must break his heart.‚Äù The words were pronounced, but the archdeacon had so much command over himself as to speak them in such a voice that the man should not hear them. But it was incumbent on him to say something that the man should hear. ‚ÄúI will have no meddling in the matter, Flurry. Whether there are foxes or whether there are not, is matter of no great moment. I will not have a word said to annoy Mr.¬ÝThorne.‚Äù Then he rode away, back through the wood and out on to the road, and the horse walked with him leisurely on, whither the archdeacon hardly knewÔªø‚Äîfor he was thinking, thinking, thinking.

‚ÄúWell;Ôªø‚Äîif that ain‚Äôt the darn‚Äôdest thing that ever was,‚Äù said Flurry; ‚Äúbut I‚Äôll tell the squire about Thorne‚Äôs manÔªø‚Äîdarned if I don‚Äôt.‚Äù Now, ‚Äúthe squire‚Äù was young Squire Gresham, the master of the East Barsetshire hounds.

But the archdeacon went on thinking, thinking, thinking. He could have heard nothing of his son to stir him more in his favour than this strong evidence of his partiality for foxes. I do not mean it to be understood that the archdeacon regarded foxes as better than active charity, or a contented mind, or a meek spirit, or than self-denying temperance. No doubt all these virtues did hold in his mind their proper places, altogether beyond contamination of foxes. But he had prided himself on thinking that his son should be a country gentleman, and probably nothing doubting as to the major‚Äôs active charity and other virtues, was delighted to receive evidence of those tastes which he had ever wished to encourage in his son‚Äôs character. Or rather, such evidence would have delighted him at any other time than the present. Now it only added more gall to his cup. ‚ÄúWhy should he teach himself to care for such things, when he has not the spirit to enjoy them,‚Äù said the archdeacon to himself. ‚ÄúHe is a foolÔªø‚Äîa fool. A man that has been married once, to go crazy after a little girl, that has hardly a dress to her back, and who never was in a drawing-room in her life! Charles is the eldest, and he shall be the eldest. It will be better to keep it together. It is the way in which the country has become what it is.‚Äù He was out nearly all day, and did not see his wife till dinnertime. Her father, Mr.¬ÝHarding, was still with them, but had breakfasted in his own room. Not a word, therefore, was said about Henry Grantly between the father and mother on that evening.

Mrs.¬ÝGrantly was determined that, unless provoked, she would say nothing to him till the following morning. He should sleep upon his wrath before she spoke to him again. And he was equally unwilling to recur to the subject. Had she permitted it, the next morning would have passed away, and no word would have been spoken. But this would not have suited her. She had his orders to write, and she had undertaken to obey these ordersÔªø‚Äîwith the delay of one day. Were she not to write at allÔªø‚Äîor in writing to send no message from the father, there would be cause for further anger. And yet this, I think, was what the archdeacon wished.

‚ÄúArchdeacon,‚Äù she said, ‚ÄúI shall write to Henry today.‚Äù

‚ÄúVery well.‚Äù

‚ÄúAnd what am I to say from you?‚Äù

‚ÄúI told you yesterday what are my intentions.‚Äù

‚ÄúI am not asking about that now. We hope there will be years and years to come, in which you may change them, and shape them as you will. What shall I tell him now from you?‚Äù

‚ÄúI have nothing to say to himÔªø‚Äînothing; not a word. He knows what he has to expect from me, for I have told him. He is acting with his eyes open, and so am I. If he marries Miss Crawley, he must live on his own means. I told him that myself so plainly, that he can want no further intimation.‚Äù Then Mrs.¬ÝGrantly knew that she was absolved from the burden of yesterday‚Äôs message, and she plumed herself on the prudence of her conduct. On the same morning the archdeacon wrote the following note:Ôªø‚Äî

Dear ThorneÔªø‚Äî

My man tells me that foxes have been trapped on Darvell‚Äôs farm, just outside the coppices. I know nothing of it myself, but I am sure you‚Äôll look to it.
